nodes.1.1.1: You can start playing from age 13. However, until age 18 you must have a supervising adult aged 18+ who takes responsibility and remains on the field (does not need to play) until the end of the session
nodes.1.4.1: Eye protection is mandatory for all players and must be impact-rated (ANSI Z87.1+). It is recommended to use full-face protection to prevent injuries to the face and teeth
nodes.2.1.1: These balls are made of plastic compressed under high pressure. However, there are also specially heavy balls where aluminum sawdust is added to the plastic to increase the ball's weight
nodes.2.2.1: Tracer BBs are balls with a coating of material that glows in the dark. They work with a tracer unit that illuminates the ball as it exits the weapon
nodes.2.3.1: Bio BBs are balls that break down in nature after use. More environmentally friendly but with drawbacks. Storage time is limited to about six months
nodes.3.1.1: Electric weapons are electro-mechanical tools powered by a motor and battery
nodes.3.2.1: Gas weapons (GBB or GBBR) are tools that use compressed gas like propane. These weapons are more realistic compared to real firearms
nodes.3.3.1: Air weapons use compressed air pressure also called HPA. These weapons are more reliable for maintenance but require additional equipment to start playing
nodes.3.4.1: Spring weapons use a spring mechanism inside the tool. Usually these are sniper rifles or cheap pistols from the flee market unrelated to the Airsoft industry
nodes.4.1.1: Stored in 12g pressurized cartridges. CO2 provides higher pressure than Green Gas, making it reliable in cold weather and offering a stronger recoil. It is more taxing on O-rings, so regular lubrication is essential.
nodes.4.2.1: Standard pressurized gas sold in large cans. It usually contains silicone oil to lubricate seals. It is easier to 'top off' a magazine than replacing a CO2 bulb, but it is very sensitive to cold temperatures.
nodes.4.3.1: Specialized Green Gas with higher pressure. Red gas is for cool weather; Black gas is for near-freezing temperatures. Warning: Never use these in high summer or in plastic-slide pistols to avoid catastrophic failure.
nodes.4.4.1:
Israel's climate dictates gas choice. The rule of thumb is to use the lowest pressure gas that still cycles your replica reliably. For most players and replicas, Green Gas is the standard choice year-round. Only switch to CO2 if the winter cold prevents your gas from cycling, and avoid using CO2 as a 'remedy' for mechanical issues.
CO2 should primarily be used if explicitly recommended by the manufacturer.
nodes.4.5.1: When switching gases or BB weights, your gun's power changes. Joule Creep occurs when heavier BBs exit the barrel with more kinetic energy (Joules) than lighter ones, even if the FPS is lower. Always chronograph with the BB weight and gas you intend to use on the field.
nodes.5.1.1: NiMH batteries are older, heavier, and safer but have slower trigger response. LiPo (Lithium Polymer) are the modern standard: they are smaller, perform better, and require a dedicated LiPo charger. Never fully deplete a LiPo.
nodes.5.2.1: 7.4V (2-cell) is the safe standard for most AEGs. 11.1V (3-cell) provides a much higher rate of fire and instant response but puts significantly more strain on the internal gears and motor.
nodes.5.3.1: Never use an 11.1V battery unless your replica has a MOSFET. Without one, the high voltage will cause 'arcing' on your trigger contacts, eventually welding them together or burning them out.
nodes.5.4.1: Form factor is key for fitment: 'Stick/Cylinder' fits in AK dust covers or tight buffer tubes; 'Nunchuck/Crane' fits in Crane stocks (split into two sides); 'Brick' is for large fixed stocks or PEQ boxes.
nodes.5.5.1: Mini-Tamiya (greenish/white) is the old standard but has high resistance. T-Plug (Deans, red/burgundy) is the modern standard, offering much lower resistance and better durability. Always check which connector your replica uses before buying.
nodes.5.6.1: The 'C' rating (e.g., 20C, 30C) indicates how fast the battery can safely push energy to the motor. Higher C means less 'sag' under load. For most builds, 20C-30C is the sweet spot.
